arrival_structure.era_2: The Keepers of the Last Tide arrive — 7 people, 1 ship, 1 god. They present themselves as scholars and refugees. They teach safe divine partnership techniques. They don't explain what they're running from.
arrival_structure.era_3: The majority Tideborn arrive — dozens of ships, hundreds of people, Makeo's descendants leading. They say they've come to retrieve their wayward kin. They're warm, skilled, generous — and the inner circle is carrying a ritual that can kill gods.

the_keepers.name: Keepers of the Last Tide
the_keepers.goal: Protect the Listener. Find allies on this continent who will help them resist the majority when they inevitably follow. Teach safe partnership techniques to build goodwill and establish trust before the truth comes out.
the_keepers.what_they_offer: Genuine divine partnership techniques. The ability to hear gods, negotiate with them, build reciprocal relationships. This is safe. This works. This is not the Unmaking.
the_keepers.what_they_hide: The Unmaking exists. Their people killed their own gods with it. The majority is coming. The Listener is the last one. Rua knows the Unmaking ritual herself — learned it so she could recognize the preparation.
the_keepers.key_figure_rua: Keeper elder. 60 years old. Carried the Listener across the ocean. Knows which of the Tideborn gods consented to the Unmaking and which didn't — she carries that list in her memory because it's the only proof of what really happened. She's the most morally compromised member of the Keepers: she learned the god-killing ritual to protect against it, which means she could perform it. She's a weapon pretending to be a shield. She knows this.
the_keepers.internal_tension: Some Keepers want to warn the continental peoples immediately. Others argue that revealing the Unmaking's existence makes it more likely someone here will try to learn it. Rua is paralyzed between both positions.

the_majority.name: The Tideborn Flotilla
the_majority.size: Hundreds, arriving in Era 3. Dozens of ships.
the_majority.goal: Retrieve the Keepers and the Listener. Publicly: 'our lost kin need to come home.' Privately: the inner circle (Makeo's descendants) wants the Listener because it's the last god they haven't consumed. The inner circle doesn't frame it as consumption — they frame it as 'completing the transcendence.' The casual members of the flotilla genuinely just want their people back.
the_majority.what_they_offer: Everything the Keepers offer plus MORE — more divine gifts on display, more impressive techniques, more people, more resources. They're dazzling. The continental factions will prefer them to the Keepers because they look stronger.
the_majority.what_they_hide: The Unmaking. The inner circle carries it. Most of the flotilla doesn't know it exists. The youth carry divine gifts in their blood and believe they're inherited blessings. The elders know those 'blessings' are the stripped essence of murdered gods.
the_majority.key_figure_descendant: Makeo's grandson. Name TBD. The flotilla's leader. Carries more divine fragments than anyone alive — he can calm storms, read deep currents, speak to marine life. He's warm, competent, and genuinely believes the gods 'chose to become part of us.' He doesn't know about the gods who didn't choose. He's the best advertisement for why the Unmaking might be transcendence rather than murder. He's also the person the inner circle is grooming to perform the Unmaking on the Listener, and he doesn't know that either.
the_majority.the_inner_circle: 5-6 elders who know the Unmaking and carry it as sacred duty. They believe they're honoring Makeo's vision. Some of them have doubts. All of them are too invested to stop. They've crossed an ocean to finish what Makeo started.

continental_impact.era_2_keepers_arrive: The continent meets the Keepers and sees: helpful strangers with amazing techniques. Trust builds. The player can form deep relationships with Rua and the Keepers. The Listener may begin communicating with continental gods. The Keepers become embedded in whatever world state exists — valuable allies in the Age of Exchange, mysterious scholars in the Age of Questions, spiritual kin to the Rootwalkers in the Age of Wandering.
continental_impact.era_3_majority_arrives: Everything changes. The Keepers' fear makes sense now. The majority arrives looking for their 'lost kin' and the continental factions must choose: hand over the Keepers (the people they've come to trust), protect them (and antagonize a much larger foreign power), or try to mediate a conflict they don't understand. The inner circle begins quietly assessing continental gods for the Unmaking.
continental_impact.the_reveal: At some point, through player investigation or Rua's confession or an inner circle member's mistake, the Unmaking becomes known. The continental reaction depends on the world state: the Age of Questions sees a theological weapon. The Age of Iron sees a military threat. The Age of Exchange sees leverage. The Rootwalkers see the death of everything they believe about divine relationships.
continental_impact.the_choice: The player must decide: is the Unmaking a tool, a weapon, a crime, or a mercy? Some continental gods might WANT it — the Ashfield god, born from fear, might prefer dissolution into a loving people over an eternity of being fed dread. The Silence might want it because silence is the endpoint of all things. The Burn might want to unmake a rival. The question isn't whether the Unmaking is evil. The question is who gets to decide.
